best_narrative: This vehicle is equipped with Lane Keep Assist feature. The posted speed limit the subject vehicle was operating at time of incident is 25 MPH and the Lane Keep Assist Feature cannot be activated at speeds below 37 MPH. This crash occurred as unit 1 was going North on [XXX] in Maxatawny Township, Berks County. Unit 1 then went off the right side of the roadway and struck a house. Unit 1 was towed from the scene. Operator 1 and homeowner were transported to nearby local hospital to be treated for minor injuries.

All of the data in this report and the following text is an excerpt from TuSimples original submission (30370-2949-1) and constitutes Navistars sole understanding of this event.
TuSimple's position is that this event does not meet the criteria for reporting under the SGO.
Specifically, the L4 ADS was not in operation at any point in the 30 seconds prior to making contact with the concrete barrier.
The event occurred as follows:
As the truck was being operated on the highway, within its mapped ODD, the driver and test engineer attempted to engage the ADS.
However, the ADS was not functional at that moment due to the computer unit not having been initialized, and should not have been
attempted to be activated. In short, this was a failed attempt to engage the system as a result of human error.
The system has now been modified to prevent the ADS from attempting to engage unless the health of the system is fully functional to
prevent human error from repeating this event.
When the erroneous attempt to engage occurred, the uninitialized vehicle control unit rotated the steering to the left, causing the truck to
veer left. The safety driver took control of the steering, and was able to steer accordingly, but not before the left front truck tire and left
front quarter panel came into contact with the concrete barrier to the left of the lanes of travel.

best_narrative:
Torc Robotics and Daimler Truck North America ("DTNA") are submitting duplicate incident reports (refer to Torc report ID: 30330-6882-1) regarding a deer strike involving a Torc-operated vehicle that occurred in Texas on December [XXX], 2023 at 14:35 CST. The vehicle's automated driving system ("ADS") was engaged within 30 seconds prior to the event. Although a deer strike does not appear to meet the SGO's definition of a crash (i.e., impact with a vehicle, other road user, or property), we are reporting this incident out of an abundance of caution and in the interest of transparency. 
At the time of the incident, the vehicle was traveling westbound in the rightmost lane of [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION]. A deer crossed the eastbound lanes of traffic and median before entering the westbound lanes.  Torc's in-vehicle fallback test driver ("IFTD"), positioned in the driver's seat, noticed the deer as it entered the westbound lanes. The IFTD took control of the vehicle by disengaging the ADS. The deer then contacted the left side of the tractor. After the physical impact with the tractor, the deer deflected into the left lane toward the left shoulder of the roadway and did not contact other road users. The IFTD reduced the speed of the vehicle and navigated to a rest area to inspect it. During the inspection, the IFTD observed minor damage on the driver's side of the tractor but deemed the vehicle roadworthy for manual operation.  The IFTD contacted local law enforcement to notify them of the event. The test crew (IFTD, safety conductor, and passenger) returned the vehicle to the Torc test operations site.

best_narrative:
- The subject vehicle of this report was procured by and up-fitted to ADS L4 by May Mobility with a May Mobility automated driving kit.
- The content of this report, including the summary of incident below, is the information as Toyota received from May Mobility on Aug [XXX], 2021.
Summary of incident (as Toyota received from May Mobility):
Leading up to the event, the autonomy system functioned as expected, following the route through the intersection, slowing appropriately for other road users and the red light. Vehicle autonomous behavior was performing as expected and did not require a takeover.
Description of event:
16:17:30 May vehicle, traveling East on [XXX], in full working order with no errors, autonomously navigates the [XXX] and [XXX] intersection.
16:17:35 May vehicle autonomously begins to slow to match the speed of a vehicle several car lengths ahead merging into our lane from the right.
16:17:37 By means of an accelerator pedal push the vehicle is moved into manual mode by the safety operator - from this point forward, the vehicle remains under control of the safety operator.
16:17:43 Manual acceleration command via the pedal increases.
16:17:44 Safety operator manually continues straight which, due to the slight change in angle of the road, brings the May vehicle into contact with the first vehicle to the right.
16:17:44 Safety operator manually applies brake.
16:17:45 With the manual brake command now at maximum the May vehicle makes contact with the second vehicle.
Vehicle indicators for manual or autonomy mode:
The visual indicator for autonomy was functioning properly and as expected in the time leading up to and during the event.
The audio communication device in the vehicle was functioning properly and was used to call for assistance and to report the incident.
The audio beep to indicate a state change was functioning properly.
The safety operator had some audio indication that he had exited autonomy and entered manual operation, and he had full visual indication on his dashboard.

The subject vehicle of this report was procured by and up-fitted to ADS L4 by May Mobility with a May Mobility automated driving kit.
The content of this report, including the summary of incident below, is the information as Toyota received it from May Mobility on Sept. [XXX],
2021.
Vehicle made a right turn and struck the curb, causing a blown tire.
Vehicle elected a controller path mid corner, which caused the curb strike. We have assessed the localities where this could occur and
are taking those turns in manual operation. We are currently testing a fix that will make our controller more aware of the curb to prevent
future occurrences.

The May vehicle was traveling on an unpainted (no center line) residential road with vehicles parked along the right side of the road. The May vehicle passed too close to a parked vehicle, causing the May vehicle's passenger-side mirror to contact the parked vehicle's driver-side mirror.
The May vehicle's ground mapping module had fallen significantly behind in its processing of lidar data. As a result, actual objects were being filtered out of the lidar data during object detection. This filtering caused the truck to oscillate between being detected, detecting only the cab, detecting the cab and mirror, and not being detected at all.
After assessing the issue, it has been determined that ground map latency alone will not cause safety issues. There is no risk to VRUs, or anything at ground level. The issue was with something in the air (mirror), above the ground.
A permanent fix to address the ground map latency issue is in process and will be implemented in the entire fleet within the next 30 days.
The second factor in the collision is that we were artificially constraining the vehicle to the right side of the road due to ambiguous and unpainted roadway. If we change that constraint to an advisory line (and allow the vehicle to cross the line to the left), we would have been far enough to the left to avoid collision (simulation success).
Solution Implementation: AVO training for extra caution related to protruding items like mirrors (short term), software changes coming in the next update and hotfix (within 30 days), and further improvement in the following update (within 60 days).
Until the tested software changes are implemented, we have advised the AVOs to take extra caution in these scenarios and to continue to disengage autonomy when desired. As always, we sacrifice autonomy for safety, as needed.

May Mobility vehicle is traveling westbound in autonomous mode on [XXX], stopped at a red light. When the traffic light turns green, the subject vehicle accelerates up to approximately 10mph. Approximately halfway through the intersection of [XXX], the subject vehicle begins to slow down due to construction fencing that is intruding into the lane, eventually coming to a complete stop.  The safety operator takes over manual operation via the accelerator pedal, but is struck from the rear by a Toyota pickup that was following. The Toyota was first struck by the Chevy vehicle traveling behind it, pushing the Toyota into the subject vehicle.

The May Mobility vehicle was traveling in autonomy through a parking lot and comes to a stop due to a vehicle backing up. Vehicle begins to reverse further so the operator in the May Mobility vehicle takes over and manually reverses. The other vehicle continues backing up and does not stop, contacting the front of the May Mobility vehicle. Other vehicle then proceeds to drive away.

Operator was approaching stop sign at the intersection of [XXX] westbound and [XXX] northbound, which is a planned disengagement (this unprotected turn is outside of our ODD). If left in autonomy, the vehicle would have come to a full stop before the stop line and remained stopped until the operator took over. 6 seconds prior to incident, operator took the vehicle over manually before it could have stopped in autonomy. Operator moved the vehicle manually passed the stop line, colliding with a bicyclist's back tire. No injuries reported from the bicyclist or the operator and no damage to the vehicle or bicycle.

This report is being submitted because May Mobility notified Toyota on Dec. 9, 2022 of 1-Day Report submission 30413-4378-1,related to an incident that occurred on Dec. [XXX], 2022. Toyota has no additional information on this incident beyond what was submitted in the aforementioned 1-Day Report. Please refer to the report from May Mobility for details.
This report is a 10-Day update. The original report ID is 1306-4390-1.

The subject vehicle was stopped at a traffic light at the corner of [XXX] and [XXX] (Austin,TX) while traveling westbound on [XXX]  While ADS was engaged, the subject vehicle was stationary, the passenger car behind the subject vehicle proceeded forward from a stationary position and bumped into the rear bumper of the subject vehicle.  The passenger vehicle reversed away from the subject vehicle. The operator of the subject vehicle then turned on the hazard lights, proceeded through the intersection once the light changed to green and turned right onto [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] in order to check for a safe spot to park.  The passenger vehicle, after reversing away from the subject vehicle, did not follow the subject vehicle and instead proceeded to turn right onto [XXX]. Once the operator pulled over they then followed protocol to call in the incident, took photos to document, and waited for manager and service to arrive at the scene to verify the damage. There were no known injuries, airbag deployments, or tow-away of anyone involved.  There was no significant damage to the subject vehicle and it was driven back to the depot to debrief and provide information to incident response team on the incident.

The subject vehicle was stopped at the intersection of [XXX] and [XXX] with the ADS system engaged, waiting for the traffic light to turn green. The passenger car (Toyota Tacoma, equipped with front grille/brush guard) approached from behind, honked the horn, and struck the stationary subject vehicle. The driver of the subject vehicle proceed through the intersection and pulled over into an empty parking lot to safely assess the incident. The passenger car is noted as remaining at the light for an additional cycle of the traffic light before driving off and not pulling over with the vehicle it just struck. The subject vehicle's 3 occupants reported feeling the impact but report no injuries, airbag deployments, or tow-aways. The subject vehicle's damage was to the right rear bumper, including the right rear sensor and mounting bracket.

The subject vehicle (autonomous vehicle- AV) was stopped, in the far right turn lane on westbound [XXX], waiting to turn right onto northbound [XXX]. The other vehicle was a 2021 Mercedes Benz GLE (MB) that was noted as being stopped prior to making contact with the rear right bumper of the subject vehicle.  The AV, while remaining stationary, was struck from the rear by the MB. The driver of the AV signaled to the other vehicle to pull over safely in the adjacent parking lot. The driver's of both vehicles exchanged information and assessed the damage. Both vehicles had one occupant and there were no reported injuries, airbag deployments, or tow-aways. The damage to the subject vehicle is stated as scratches/markings of the right rear bumper, and the damage to the other vehicle is a bent frontal license plate. Both vehicles were able to safely leave the scene.  VWGoA does not plan to investigate the incident any further as the AV was within its ODD at the time of the incident and was struck from behind while waiting for traffic to clear to safely complete a turn.

The subject vehicle (autonomous vehicle - AV) was proceeding through a parking garage while engaged in autonomous driving (AD) mode.  The AV detected an oncoming passenger vehicle (PV), made adjustments to its route by moving to the right, and slowed to a stop to provide additional space for the PV.  As the PV passed, it attempted to make a left-hand turn behind the AV, but the PV's driver side mirror scraped the rear driver's side LiDAR housing of the AV.  The passenger vehicle continued driving without stopping. After noticing the contact, the safety driver of the subject vehicle manually disengaged AD mode to assess the incident.  There were no reported injuries, airbag deployments, or tow-aways.  The damage to the subject vehicle was a scuff to the driver's side Short Range LiDAR trim cover.  VWGoA does not plan to investigate the incident any further as the facts and circumstances do not indicate any issue with the vehicle's performance.

The subject vehicle (autonomous vehicle - AV) was stopped at a red traffic light, in a queue of vehicles, heading west on [XXX], with autonomous driving (AD) mode engaged. Once the light turned green, the queue of vehicles started to move forward, the AV started to move forward with the queue, and prior to crossing the intersection, the AV was struck from behind by an SUV. After impact the safety driver of the AV applied the brakes to disengage AD mode and then proceeded to find a safe spot to assess the incident by turning right onto [XXX]. The driver of the SUV followed the AV to a safe location to assess the incident and exchange contact information. There were no reported injuries, airbag deployments, or tow-aways between either party involved.  The AV sustained minor damage to the rear of the vehicle including the rear bumper cover, rear bumper carrier, and a rear facing camera. The SUV damage was reported as scratches and dents to the front bumper cover. Both vehicles were able to safely drive away from the scene. VWGoA does not plan to investigate the incident any further as the facts and circumstances do not indicate any issue with the vehicle's performance.

The initial submission was by email and electronic form to SGO Mailbox on 10/18/2022 at 10:09PM.  This is a resubmission of data using the SGO portal with Volvo Cars USA, LLC account on behalf of Polestar Automotive USA. Volvo Cars USA, LLC is submitting this report on behalf of Polestar Automotive USA and Polestar Performance AB.  The vehicle was a company vehicle (owned by Polestar Automotive USA) driven by a company employee, with no known modifications to the factory hardware condition or software updates or changes not released to the public. Subject vehicle was traveling around a curve on the freeway interchange at approximately 55mph, recommended speed in that portion of exchange was 50mph.  Driver looked down and took attention off the road.  The vehicle continued slightly straight, running up and grazing off the concrete barrier, before coming back into the lane.  The vehicle's rear suspension, rear and front wheel/tire were damaged. Vehicle was towed from the scene. We have recovered data from the vehicle and are currently reviewing what information was stored for the incident and will update this report.
10 Day update: After review of the EDR data the technical team was able to determine the following: Pilot Assist was disengaged due to the lateral acceleration exceeded the system limit and the available steering torque system input exceeding the limit.  The vehicle could not steer enough to follow the lane. Several warnings were issued: visually in the DIM (Drivers Information Module, display), haptically in the steering wheel, before Pilot Assist was disengaged. The driver is always responsible for ensuring that the vehicle is driven in a safe manner. The driver failed to take control of the vehicle when the vehicles Pilot Assist gave the warnings.

best_narrative:
On September [XXX], 2025 at 1:21 AM PT a Waymo Autonomous Vehicle ("Waymo AV") operating in Tempe, Arizona was in a collision involving a motorcycle on [XXX] at [XXX]. 
The Waymo AV was traveling north in the rightmost lane on [XXX] and began slowing with its right turn signal activated in preparation for a right turn into a parking lot at [XXX], yielding to a pedestrian crossing the driveway entrance to the parking lot on the right-hand sidewalk. While the Waymo AV was slowing with its right turn signal activated, a motorcycle in the rightmost lane approached the Waymo AV from behind and the front of the motorcycle made contact with the rear left corner of the Waymo AV. The impact with the Waymo AV resulted in the motorcyclist falling from the motorcycle and into the roadway to the left of the Waymo AV. Immediately afterwards, a passenger car in the second-from-the-rightmost lane passed the Waymo AV on the left and struck the motorcyclist in the roadway. The passenger car did not stop and left the scene. The motorcyclist was transported from the scene to a hospital, where they were declared deceased. At the time of impact, the Waymo AV's Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode, and the Waymo AV was not carrying passengers. Following contact with the motorcycle, the Waymo AV came to a complete stop and remained stopped until the vehicle was towed away several hours later. The Waymo AV and the motorcycle sustained damage. The extent of the damage to the passenger car that left the scene is unknown at this time.
Waymo is reporting this crash under Request No. 1 of Standing General Order 2021-01 because of a fatality. Waymo may supplement or correct its reporting with additional information as it may become available.

best_narrative:
On October [XXX], 2025 at 7:48 AM MT a Waymo Autonomous Vehicle ("Waymo AV") operating in Phoenix, Arizona was in a collision involving a passenger car on [XXX] at [XXX]. 
The Waymo AV was stopped facing north in the rightmost lane on [XXX] in a queue of traffic for a red light at the intersection at [XXX]. A passenger car traveling northbound in the rightmost lane on [XXX] slowed to a stop directly behind the Waymo AV. After the traffic light turned green and traffic ahead advanced, the Waymo AV proceeded a short distance and came to a stop before entering the intersection, as it detected a siren from an emergency vehicle. The passenger car also proceeded forward initially and then came to a stop to yield to the Waymo AV. While the Waymo AV and the passenger car remained stopped, a second passenger car traveling northbound on [XXX] approached the first passenger car from behind and the front of the second passenger car made contact with the rear of the first passenger car. The front of the first passenger car then made contact with the rear of the stationary Waymo AV. At the time of the impact, the Waymo AV's Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode. The Waymo AV, the first passenger car, and the second passenger car sustained damage.

Waymo is reporting this crash under Request No. 1 of Standing General Order 2021-01 because a vehicle involved was towed away. Waymo may supplement or correct its reporting with additional information as it may become available.

On October [XXX], 2025 at 9:11 AM PT a Waymo Autonomous Vehicle ("Waymo AV") operating in San Francisco, California was stopped in-lane when a collision occurred between an SUV and a pickup truck on [XXX] South near the [XXX] on-ramp.
The Waymo AV was traveling north on [XXX] near the intersection with [XXX]. The Waymo AV slowed and continued past the intersection with [XXX] and continued onto the [XXX] South on-ramp at [XXX]. The Waymo AV came to a stop at the stop sign at the entrance to [XXX] South and then proceeded approximately 30 meters in the right-most lane of [XXX] South and came to a stop in a location with no right-hand shoulder. The Waymo AV remained stopped and four vehicles (two buses, two passenger vehicles) entered [XXX] South from the [XXX] on-ramp and passed the Waymo AV on the left. After the Waymo AV had been stopped for a period of two minutes and 18 seconds, a white SUV entered [XXX] South from the [XXX] on-ramp. After the SUV proceeded past the stop sign onto [XXX] South, it merged into the lane to the left of the Waymo AV. As the SUV entered the lane to the left of the Waymo AV, the front left corner of the SUV made contact with the right side of a pickup truck traveling in the lane to the left of the Waymo AV. The pickup truck then lost control, traveling into the right lane and through the barrier on the rightmost side of the bridge carrying [XXX] over [XXX], then fell to the roadway below. The SUV came to a stop and remained stopped in the lane to the left of the Waymo AV. The passenger of the Waymo AV exited the vehicle after the collision. The Waymo AV proceeded on [XXX] South in autonomous mode.

At the time of the impact between the SUV and pickup truck, the Waymo AV's Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode. The SUV and pickup truck sustained damage. Shortly after the collision, Waymo received notice that minor injuries were reported by passengers of the pickup truck.

Waymo is reporting this crash under Request No. 1 of Standing General Order 2021-01 because a vehicle involved was towed away. Waymo may supplement or correct its reporting with additional information as it may become available.

On October [XXX], 2025 at 11:40 PM PT a Waymo Autonomous Vehicle ("Waymo AV") operating in San Francisco, California made contact with a cat on [XXX] at [XXX]. 
The Waymo AV was stopped next to the curb for a passenger pickup facing east on [XXX]. As the passengers were boarding the Waymo AV, a cat approached the Waymo AV from the southern sidewalk of [XXX] and sat in the roadway partially under the front right corner of the Waymo AV. A pedestrian approached the Waymo AV from the east on the southern sidewalk of [XXX] and began crouching near the front of the Waymo AV, stepping partially into the roadway, appearing to reach for the cat. As they did so, the cat moved farther from the sidewalk under the Waymo AV and the pedestrian stepped back onto the sidewalk. The Waymo AV then departed the pickup location and the rear right tire made contact with the cat. At the time of impact, the Waymo AV's Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode. Waymo later received notice that the cat did not survive. The passengers in the Waymo AV did not have seatbelts fastened at the time, having just boarded the Waymo AV.

Waymo is reporting this crash under Request 2 of Standing General Order 2021-01. Waymo may supplement or correct its reporting with additional information as it may become available.

While the AV was in  motion, the two passengers seated in the rear of the Waymo AV removed their seatbelts and opened the right rear passenger side door. The Waymo AV detected the opened door and began to slow in lane. While the Waymo AV was slowing to a stop, the passenger seated in the right rear passenger seat exited the vehicle through the open door, placed their feet on the ground, and the rear right tire of the Waymo AV came to a stop on top of the passenger's right foot. At the time of the impact, the Waymo AV's Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode. The passenger of the Waymo AV was transported from the scene to a hospital for medical treatment with moderate injuries to the foot.

On December [XXX], 2025 at 1:17 PM PT a Waymo Autonomous Vehicle ("Waymo AV") operating in Los Angeles, California was in a collision involving a van on [XXX] near [XXX].
The Waymo AV was stopped facing west on [XXX] for a red light at the intersection with [XXX] with its right turn signal activated. After remaining stopped for approximately six minutes a short distance in advance of the stop line at the red light, the Waymo AV passenger opened the rear left side door and exited the vehicle, and a van that was initially stopped directly behind the Waymo AV maneuvered to the left before slowing to a stop behind the Waymo AV. The Waymo AV passenger briefly interacted with the van operator through the van window, and then re-entered the Waymo AV. The van then proceeded to pass the Waymo AV on the left and the right side of the van made contact with the rear left side of the stationary Waymo AV. At the time of the impact, the Waymo AV's Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode. Both vehicles sustained damage. The passenger in the Waymo AV was not belted at the time of the collision.

Waymo is reporting this crash under Request No. 2 of Standing General Order 2021-01. Waymo may supplement or correct its reporting with additional information as it may become available.

On June [XXX], 2022 at approximately 4:50 PM PST a Waymo Autonomous Vehicle (Waymo AV) operating in San Francisco, California allegedly contributed to a collision between a passenger vehicle and a pickup truck on [XXX] at [XXX], according to a claim by the driver of the passenger vehicle.

The Waymo AV proceeded to merge onto [XXX] as a passenger vehicle on [XXX] approached from behind. A pickup truck was traveling next to the passenger vehicle in the adjacent left lane. The Waymo AV test driver then transitioned to manual mode and pulled over. Waymo has no data or knowledge of any contact between any of the three vehicles. There was no contact or collision between the Waymo AV and any other vehicle. However, the driver and passenger in the passenger vehicle have subsequently alleged injuries based on alleged contact between the pick-up truck and passenger vehicle, which contact their allegations attribute to the behavior of Waymos AV in a bodily injury claim under Waymos insurance policy.  Waymo received notice of this insurance claim on July [XXX].

On January [XXX], 2023 at 1:38 PM PST a Waymo Autonomous Vehicle (Waymo AV) operating in Phoenix, Arizona was in a collision with a minibike whose rider was no longer on it on [XXX] at [XXX].
The Waymo AV was traveling in the leftmost lane on eastbound [XXX], with a group of smaller motorcycles, more commonly known as minibikes, traveling in the right adjacent lane next to and ahead of the Waymo AV. A minibike rider in the group lost control and fell off the minibike a short distance ahead of the Waymo AV in the adjacent lane and the minibike, without a rider, tumbled into the Waymo AV's lane. The Waymo AV applied maximum brake force but made contact with the riderless minibike just before coming to a stop. At the time of the impact, the Waymo AVs Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode. The Waymo AV sustained damage. The minibike was damaged but it is not possible to determine whether all or some of that damage occurred when the minibike fell to the road before the collision. Because we checked on the minibike riders well-being shortly after the crash, Waymo is aware that the individual sustained minor injuries when they fell from the minibike but the subsequent collision with the riderless minibike did not cause those injuries.
Although we do not believe this event falls under Request No. 1 of Standing General Order 2021-01, we consulted NHTSA and submitted this report at the agencys request on the day of the consultation. Waymo may supplement or correct its reporting with additional information as it may become available.

On February [XXX], 2024 at 2:34 PM PT a Waymo Autonomous Vehicle (Waymo AV) operating in San Francisco, California was in a collision involving a bicyclist on [XXX] at [XXX].

When it was the Waymo AVs turn at the intersection, the Waymo AV entered the intersection to proceed straight. At this point, a delivery truck traveling eastbound on [XXX] was already in the middle of the intersection (the delivery truck arrived at its stop sign first and proceeded into the intersection before the Waymo AV). As the Waymo AV passed the rear of the delivery truck, an occluded bicyclist who was traveling behind the delivery truck became visible and was making a sharp left turn onto northbound [XXX], in front of the Waymo AVs path. The cyclist had entered the 4-way stop intersection without stopping or yielding. The Waymo AV detected the bicyclist, applied heavy braking, but was unable to avoid contact with the bicyclist before coming to a full stop. 
At the time of the impact, the Waymo AVs Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode. The Waymo AV sustained minor body damage, and the bicyclist reported minor injuries and damage to their bicycle. The Waymo AV remained stopped in the intersection for approximately one minute, and after the path was clear, resumed driving for a short period of time. The Waymo AV was then returned to the scene by one of Waymos roadside assistance specialists. The Waymo AV did not require towing and was returned to the depot in manual mode.
Waymo is reporting this crash under Request No. 1 of Standing General Order 2021-01 because a vulnerable road user was involved. Waymo may supplement or correct its reporting with additional information as it may become available.

The cyclist then reached out a hand and made contact with the front passenger side of the stationary Waymo AV, backed the bicycle up slightly, dropped the bicycle, then fell to the ground. 
At the time of the possible impact, the Waymo AV's Level 4 ADS was engaged in autonomous mode, and a test driver was present. The cyclist received medical attention at the scene and was reportedly transported to a hospital by local emergency services. The Waymo AV did not sustain any damage. 
Even though this event may involve intentional contact by the bicyclist with a stationary Waymo AV and the occurrence of actual impact between the Waymo AV and the cycle is not clear, Waymo is reporting this event as a crash under Request No. 1 of Standing General Order 2021-01 because a media report included an allegation of a crash and because a vulnerable road user (cyclist) influenced the driving task of a vehicle involved and reportedly was transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the event. The Waymo AV was traveling in the rightmost lane of two northbound lanes on [XXX] near the intersection with [XXX] when it encountered the scene of a prior collision between a passenger car and an SUV. The two vehicles were stopped in the rightmost lane. The Waymo AV came to a stop behind the passenger car and a queue of traffic in the left lane began passing the scene. While the Waymo AV was stopped, occupants of the SUV and the passenger car exited their vehicles, apparently to inspect damage and/or exchange information. Motional submits this 10 Day update following continued investigation of the event. On May [XXX], 2025, at 8:25 PM PT, a Motional AV with VIN [XXX] was operating in autonomous mode in Las Vegas, Nevada. A safety driver was in the driver's seat, and a test engineer was in the passenger seat.
The AV was traveling westbound on [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] and was making a left turn onto the leftmost lane of two lanes on southbound [XXX] at a green light. In the further of the two lanes, there was a stopped construction vehicle with a construction worker walking around the outside of his vehicle. The Motional AV had the green light and right of way, and the southbound traffic had a red light. A group of cyclists on southbound [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION], ignored the red light signal and began traveling southbound towards the AV located in the intersection and attempting to complete its left hand turn. The cyclists entered the intersection while the AV was partway through completing its turn. Once the AV perceived the cyclists ignoring the red light signal and moving on a trajectory towards the AV, it braked and came to a stop, maintaining distance with the construction worker outside his vehicle and on the other side of the intersection. One cyclist (the first cyclist of the group to pass through the intersection) made contact with the stopped AV but did not fall, stop, or sustain any injury. The cyclist then continued riding along with and caught up to the larger group of cyclists.
Following the contact, the safety driver assumed control of the vehicle and safely pulled over to the side of the road to initiate Motional's incident response protocol. No damage was sustained, and there were no injuries. Law enforcement was not contacted.

On October [XXX], 2025, at approximately 2:30 PM PT, a Motional AV with VIN [XXX] was operating in autonomous mode in Las Vegas, Nevada, with a safety driver in the drivers seat. The vehicle was traveling northbound on [XXX] in the second of four lanes from the left, approaching the intersection with [XXX].
 
While stopped at a red traffic signal, an adult male pedestrian began crossing [XXX] from the west side of the street, several hundred feet away from a designated crosswalk. The pedestrian proceeded southbound across multiple lanes and paused on a median located ahead and to the left of the AV. The AV detected and classified the pedestrian the entire time.
 
When the traffic signal turned green, the pedestrian remained stopped on the median. Given that the pedestrian remained stopped in place, the AV began to move forward slowly at approximately 2.5 mph and continued to correctly detect and classify the pedestrian. The pedestrian, holding what appears to be an item in a plastic bag, suddenly stepped into the far-left turn lane ahead of the AV, the AV immediately came to a complete stop still in the second  lane. The pedestrian continued crossing, making a light physical contact with the rear driverâs side of the vehicle before continuing across the remaining three lanes of traffic still outside of the crosswalk.
 
The vehicle operator then disengaged the autonomous system and pulled over to complete an incident report. No police were notified. There were no injuries, no damage, and no airbag deployments. The pedestrian did not stop at the scene and the identity of the pedestrian is unknown.

On June 23, 2023, Motional informed HMNA's NASO of a crash incident occurring on June [XXX], 2023, at 9:42 PM PDT, involving a Hyundai IONIQ 5 test vehicle operating in proprietary ADS autonomous mode with a safety driver at the wheel and a safety operator in the front passenger seat.  According to Motional, the test vehicle was travelling northbound in the left-most of two left turn lanes on a local road at approximately 10 mph.  The test vehicle then braked for an approaching taxi in the adjacent lane that appeared to be crossing into the Motional test vehicle's lane and was rear-ended by a silver Toyota from behind.  The Toyota vehicle fled the scene of the accident.
Hyundai understands that the equipment and software used during the test is proprietary to Motional and not used as OEM equipment in any Hyundai/Genesis vehicle produced for sale to the general public.  Hyundai currently does not offer an ADS feature on any vehicle offered for sale in the U.S. market.

The braking occurred upon approach of a black SUV traveling north, quickly exiting the garage driveway toward [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION].  The involved SUV failed to stop at a stop sign at the garage exit.  After passing the stop sign the SUV appeared to decelerate but may not have come to a complete stop before traversing the crosswalk and entering [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION]. The SUV then entered [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] and struck the rear right side of the vehicle causing damage to the panel and rear bumper of the Motional vehicle.  The Motional vehicle was towed from the scene as a precaution despite being drivable. No injuries were reported, and law enforcement was not involved.  It is Hyundai's understanding that Motional is has submitted a new report of this incident under current SGO requirements [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION]. A large semi-trailer truck is making a left turn from a street on the right into the oncoming lane in Detroit, MI.  As the May autonomous shuttle (Ego) is slowing, the Autonomous Vehicle Operator (AVO) intervenes to stop earlier.  The manually controlled Ego remains stationary for five seconds.  The AVO then manually reverses two meters to create more space for the trailer intruding into the Egos lane, making contact with a rear vehicle at 0.6 m/s.  Our team looked at the incident and determined that this event was caused by human error - no autonomy software defects led to this incident.

best_narrative: The May autonomous vehicle was travelling in an autonomous vehicle (AV) only lane, on [XXX], approaching the intersection at [XXX]  A vehicle travelling to the left of the May shuttle in the non-AV lane attempted to perform a right hand turn across the May vehicles path and collided with the May AV.  The primary factor that led to this collision was human error on the part of the other vehicle that was required to yield to through traffic in the AV lane but did not.  Right turns across a straight lane are uncommon, and drivers are typically not used to them, however there is a metal street sign that instructs other traffic to yield to the vehicles in the AV lane.  There were no autonomy faults or errors which led to this incident.  Additionally, there is a second traffic light at this intersection that warns public drivers to yield to the AV shuttle. This light was not operating as it should during this incident. We are working with our partner to maintain the operation of this light and have implemented a mitigation policy of daily inspections of this light and implementing an AVO in service if this light is not functioning.

best_narrative: While traveling north on [XXX], the Autonomous Vehicle Operator (AVO) engages autonomy 3.0 m before the planned right turn and 7.7 m before the break in the curb while traveling at 8.0 m/s.  The May Autonomous Vehicle (ego) was planning to make a tight right turn into a parking lot, which the AVO was unaware of when engaging autonomy.  The ego jerked to the right upon entering autonomy, hitting a curb and damaging both passenger-side front and rear tires and the right rear tire rim.  The AV attempted the right turn despite not having time to slow sufficiently and mounted the curb.  The AVO intervened by accelerating and steering back to the left upon striking the curb, manually maneuvering back to the street while striking a second curb on the opposite side of the parking lot driveway.  Resimulations without the manual intervention show the ego slowing appropriately and making the right turn into the parking lot.  The AVO should have been attentive to the planned route before engaging autonomy.  As a result of this incident, planned improvements to the AVOs instructive dash and audio alerts for upcoming turns have begun to be implemented.

best_narrative: During normal autonomous operation, the May autonomous shuttle (Ego) was traveling through a narrow section of roadway in a parking lot bordered by parked vehicles on the left and a retaining wall on the right on experimental software version v29.0.20. As the Ego proceeded, the system initially recognized the static retaining wall as a large, non-rectangular object. As the Ego approaches the wall, the resulting density of lidar returns on the object (wall) exceeds the detectors maximum limitation. The newer lidar points, toward the forward edge of the elongated object, exceeding the limit were filtered out, causing the track to disappear when the ego approached 5m from the track (i.e. the wall). The situation was exacerbated by the fact that the road edge was incorrectly marked in that section.  The road edge was drawn through the retaining wall and thus the vehicle did not have any prior knowledge about the edge of the road. As a result, the Ego drove too close to the wall, scraping it at a speed of 2 meters per second while driving straight (i.e. in lane-nominal policy) with no nudge or veer corrections. There was no damage to the wall. The limitation leading to the lost track affects Software versions v26.0 (when the Open World Tracklet v2 feature was introduced) through v31.0. This is the only known instance of this failure mode, and in v32.0 an optimization was made to remove the LIDAR threshold entirely and mitigate the vulnerability.  Furthermore, the incorrect road edge annotation was corrected shortly after this incident and the map updated for the deployed vehicles.

best_narrative: The May Mobility vehicle (Ego) is stopped at a red light, then the Ego begins to accelerate and turn right when the traffic light changes to green. As the Ego accelerates, a pedestrian begins moving and enters the crosswalk from the other side of the intersection. This activates the rule-based crosswalk logic. Ego determines that it is able to stop before the crosswalk without exceeding our max deceleration threshold, which triggers an in-policy stopping reaction for the crosswalk. The Ego brakes to yield for the pedestrian. The vehicle trailing the Ego accelerates, making contact with the rear-end of the Ego. In the 2.5 seconds between the start of the Ego braking and the collision, the rear vehicle accelerated from 0.2 to 6.1 m/s (0.4 to 13.6 mph) without slowing until after the collision occurred. In response to this incident, immediate mitigations were made, in that the vehicle operators (AVOs) operate all intersections in manual mode.  This remained in place until January 12, when we received a solution which instituted a reduction of the 3.5 m/s2 parameter, in order to prevent the Ego from yielding to pedestrians in crosswalks that are still far from the Egoâs lane if such yielding would induce braking.

best_narrative: The May Mobility vehicle (Ego) approaches a turn with a trailing vehicle following closely behind. The May planner initially selects a trajectory it believes will safely complete the turn. However, as the Ego begins the turn, it re-evaluates the situation and detects a significant risk of striking the curb. At that point, the planner shifts focus from avoiding the curb entirely to minimizing the severity of a potential curb strike. To reduce speed before the expected contact, the Ego brakes at 3.5 m/sÂ²). Because the trailing vehicle is following so closely, this deceleration makes a rear-end collision likely. The Ego predicts a potential rear-end collision and elects to reduce brake force to lessen the impact severity. Moments later, the trailing vehicle strikes the rear driver-side bumper of the Ego and then drives away. Future improvements are underway to give the planner a policy option to steer away from curbs. This new capability will help the vehicle avoid situations where braking becomes the only remaining way to manage close curb proximity.

best_narrative: On January [XXX], 2026 at 7:48 AM, a May Mobility Autonomous Vehicle (AV) was in a collision with a fire hydrant in Atlanta, Georgia on [XXX]near[XXX]. The May Mobility AV was traveling westbound on [XXX]in Atlanta, Georgia with its automated driving system engaged in autonomy mode when the Autonomous Vehicle Operator (AVO) fell asleep while his right hand rested on the right side of the steering wheel. In-cabin video and telematics confirms that the AVO's hand restricted the motion of the steering wheel, preventing the autonomous system from keeping the vehicle in its lane. Though the AV braked after perceiving the exit from its travel lane, collision with the fire hydrant could not be avoided. The air bags deployed during the collision and the AV was towed from the scene. The AVO was transported by ambulance to the hospital with minor injuries.

Operator turned onto [XXX] headed westbound approaching the stop sign at the intersection with [XXX]. In autonomy, the subject vehicle came to a stop. An SUV behind the subject vehicle then rear-ended the subject vehicle. No injuries were reported by the operator or the driver of the other vehicle and there was no damage to the SUV and a small crack in the bumper for the subject vehicle.

On Friday April [XXX] at 16:16 local time, the May shuttle was driving Westbound on [XXX] in Ann Arbor, MI in autonomous mode and was followed by another vehicle. The following vehicle was traveling approximately twice as fast as the May shuttle and catching up to the May shuttle from behind. The May shuttle began to slow for traffic ahead, which was stopping for a red light, but the following vehicle did not decelerate. While slowing, the May shuttle identified a bollard on the side of the road as a likely pedestrian and added a safety buffer which intersected with the predicted trajectory of the shuttle. This caused the shuttle to increase deceleration to avoid the projected collision with the buffer. The following vehicle started braking late and impacted the May shuttle from behind with a relative velocity of 6.2 m/s. Re-simulation of this event with a future software version shows that the May shuttle would have decelerated less abruptly.

On Tuesday May [XXX] at 16:40 local time, the May shuttle was driving Northbound from [XXX] to [XXX] in Grand Rapids, MN, in autonomous mode and was followed by 2 people riding on scooters. As the May shuttle made the turn, the VRUs were traveling in the same direction as the May shuttle on opposite sides of the lane. As the May shuttle completed the turn at 10 MPH, the brakes were applied and the vehicle came to a complete stop because one of the riders attempted to cross over in the lane which caused VRU safety buffer to intersect with vehicle path, which led the vehicle to slow to a stop. The scooter rider contacted the rear left rear bumper of the shuttle. Scooters were traveling at an estimated speed of 12 mph and accelerating.

The May vehicle came to a gentle stop at the railroad crossing (about -0.8 m/s2 deceleration) as designed, but the agent behind did not come to a stop and made contact with the AV. The May shuttle behaved as expected and slowed down to a stop in a safe manner. The collision was unavoidable from the shuttle side.

The vehicle operator took the vehicle out of autonomy while driving NB on [XXX]. In manual the operator clipped the May vehicle's right sensor with the trunk of a diagonally parked car. The operator did not report the incident, however based on video footage they were aware of physical damage to the May vehicle. Local staff noticed a vehicle health error regarding that sensor and called the operator back to base, where they saw the damage. The operator was sent home immediately and terminated upon video review due to not following proper procedure.

The May vehicle was traveling eastbound on [XXX] in autonomy. The vehicles internal facing cameras show that the operator started to fall asleep while applying physical input to the steering wheel. The road started to bend to the left. The physical input from the operator was enough to deviate the vehicle from its intended path but not enough to trigger an intervention and take the vehicle out of autonomy. The vehicle, going straight as the road curved, then struck the curb, resulting in minor vehicle damage. Due to the vehicle being off path, the vehicle begins to stop. This occurs as the operator wakes up. The operator was not injured and no third parties were impacted or otherwise affected by the incident. 
The local May Mobility site staff did not notice any signs of fatigue from this operator prior to going out in the vehicle. The operator had responded to audio check-ins from site staff at a regular interval. We are exploring additional risk mitigation options for in-cabin health monitoring, in addition to the existing cameras and radio check-ins.

The May vehicle was in autonomous mode on experimental test software (not deployed to the Commercial fleet), in light snow conditions.  A vehicle behind the AV was traveling approximately the same speed as the May shuttle. The May AV began to slow for an object ahead. Due to weather conditions, the AV misclassified a speed limit sign as a pedestrian, which intersected with the predicted trajectory of the AV. This caused the May AV to apply additional braking to avoid the projected collision with the misclassified object. The following vehicle did not react promptly when the May AV started to slow down, resulting in the following vehicle braking too late to avoid collision and impacting the May AV from behind.  The following vehicle then pulled around the May vehicle and drove away.  Resimulation of this event with an upcoming software version shows that the May AV would not misidentify the non-exist object so would not have decelerated unnecessarily. This improved software is set to be deployed to the full commercial fleet soon. In addition, per May policy, the commercial fleet would not operate in weather conditions like those present in this incident.

The May vehicle was traveling through an intersection with a van in the oncoming left turn lane. As the May vehicle enters the intersection, the van creeps towards its turn.  Predicting that the two will get too close, the May vehicle elects to stop hard. As vehicle is coming to a stop in the stop-hard policy, the turning van fails to respond in time and collides with the rear left corner of the vehicle.
In the new software, deploying within the next month, agent-proximity has been replaced with agent-clearance. Performing resimulations of the incident with the latest branch verifies that this issue does not persist.

While in traffic and stopped at 0mph in autonomy, a motorized scooter riding between the 2 lanes of traffic clipped the May Mobility vehicle's mirror with their own mirror. The scooter proceeded along its way, seemingly unaware of the mirror clip.

May vehicle was parked in autonomous mode at a rider stop when a VRU on a bicycle approached from the north, coming towards the vehicle. Bicycle was on the wrong side of the road and appeared to be weaving. There were no other vehicles nearby and the bicycle had significant room to avoid the May vehicle. However, the bicyclist came into contact with the driver side of the May vehicle, resulting in some minor damage to the May vehicle. After contact, the bicyclist appeared uninjured, got up and left the scene on the bicycle.

May vehicle was waiting at the red light of [XXX] and [XXX]. The May vehicle was stopped and was 2nd behind the stop line. The van behind the May vehicle was also stopped. Van driver removed their foot from the brake pedal, idling into the May vehicle at a low rate of speed.

The May vehicle is approaching a lane change. It signals the lane change appropriately early and begins to gradually decelerate for the change. The trailing SUV continues to accelerate while the May vehicle is simultaneously decelerating, and eventually changes lanes directly into the May vehicle. Trailing SUV drives away without slowing meaningfully, disappearing around an upcoming turn. May vehicle deceleration never exceeds -1.3m/s2, so there is no plausible reason a responsible driver would not have had adequate time to adjust their speed or path to avoid a collision.

A May autonomous vehicle was traveling in the right lane of a two-lane road, maintaining a steady speed of 30 mph, in compliance with the posted speed limit. The road was characterized by light traffic, with a slight curvature that transitioned from a gentle rightward sway to a leftward sway. As the vehicle navigated this transition of curvature, the May vehicle made contact with the curb, causing a flat tire. 
At the time of the incident, no other vehicles or pedestrians were present in the immediate vicinity, and there were no external factors such as obstructions or adverse weather conditions influencing the vehicles path.
Speed was reduced from 30mph to 25mph after this event and tested (and worked to mitigate the problem in that area) and now will be reduced to 22 which is the sweet spot for most areas on the route. The May vehicle chose to maneuver at the curb due to perceived risk from a car in an adjacent lane, so May is increasing the allowed distance to the curb to mitigate how close the vehicles are allowed to get.

The May Mobility vehicle was at a designated pick up location waiting for a passenger to get on board. Because of an unprotected turn required to get to the stop, the autonomous vehicle operator took over manually. As the vehicle waited parked at the stop, a car in a parking space in front of the stop backed up into the May vehicle at 2mph. This impact occurs 25 seconds after last being in autonomous mode.

While traveling northbound on[XXX] in Martinez, a car pulled out of a driveway on the right side without right of way and collided with the right side of May's vehicle (t-bone.)
May's vehicle was traveling at approximately 20 MPH in a residential neighborhood, and was approximately 1m left of lane center when a black vehicle exited a driveway on the right from behind a fence, facing the road and May's vehicle. This vehicle did not slow down or stop until entering into the roadway and collided with the May vehicle's right side. AVO attempts to swerve to the left but is unable to avoid a collision. 
The primary factor that led to the collision was the other vehicles action proceeding without the right-of-way into the side of the May vehicle. Upon further inspection, it was determined that we did not predict this collision until it was too late to avoid it even by veering. The velocity of the other vehicle was underestimated and was largely given only yield policies as having the most significant likelihood. There is some room for improvement in our response, which we plan to implement in future software releases, namely, an emergency veer policy must exist that is capable of moving quickly and far enough to possibly avoid the collision, more accurate reporting of velocity for slow-moving agents, estimate acceleration when assessing risk, improvements to predictions of the probability of an agent yielding, and adjusting our risk assessment so that it is heading dependent and more risk would be associated with passing close in front of another vehicle.

The May vehicle was involved in a rear-end collision shortly after completing a right-hand turn. As the vehicle proceeded through its turn, it encountered exhaust fumes from vehicles waiting at a stoplight in the adjacent lane. Due to environmental conditions and sensor limitations, the vehicle's lidar system interpreted the exhaust plume as a solid object in its path. In response, the autonomous vehicle first tried to adjust its position. When this maneuver proved insufficient, the system initiated a hard stop response. The vehicle behind, which was following at approximately 7-8 meters, was unable to stop in time and collided with the rear of the autonomous vehicle. The collision occurred at relatively low speeds (the autonomous vehicle had decelerated from approximately 7.6 m/s to 0 m/s), though exact impact speeds of the following vehicle were not recorded. The primary factor that led to this incident is that Lidar does not pierce through exhaust so the exhaust cloud appears to be a large, solid object. Some corrective actions that will be implemented: consider weather implication in future platform sensor selection and continue working on the software to improve a neural network to classify lidar points, which will use temporal information, radar, and camera features to make a decision on whether a lidar point is truly an object.

A May shuttle began a test shift on experimental software in a parking sub-lot. The vehicle exited the sub-lot and rolled over a curb. The vehicle stopped at a stop sign at the exit of the main lot. As it proceeded to turn onto the main road, it rolled over another curb. Almost immediately after rolling the curb, the vehicle began to drift right in lane, rubbing the vehicle sidewall on a curb. The test driver intervened immediately, but not before the sidewall of the rear right tire was weakened. Later during the drive while under manual control, the sidewall fails, eventually leading to a low pressure indicator illuminating in the dash. It was discovered that the deployment of map files, which must remain compatible, was incomplete, leaving a mismatch in the system. This resulted in the vehicle having an incorrect estimate of where lanes are in the coordinates of our localization map, and as a result, the vehicle had an incorrect estimate of proximity to lane boundaries, resulting in curb strikes.

Shuttle P115 (Vin#VG9A2CB2CIV[XXX]) Shuttle was operating autonomously on route when it approached a pallet in the roadway. The shuttle made contact with the pallet prior to the attendant stopping the shuttle.According to Beep, the pallet height was just below that of the bottom most front LiDAR sensor.no severe damage, only light scratches on the underside of the shuttle grill.Beep was not able to provide any additional information, they did not take any photos of the pallet or UI events interface at the time of the incident.

A Ghost autonomous vehicle was hit by crash debris while traveling on [XXX].  The Ghost vehicle neither hit nor was hit by another vehicle.  The Ghost car was traveling in AV mode with two safety drivers.  Traffic slowed quickly, apparently due to a crash ahead of the Ghost car.  Consistent with their training, the Ghost driver took control of the car by manually braking and disengaging the autonomous system.  The car was traveling 55.3 mph when the driver began to brake.  The Ghost car stopped without impacting the preceding car.  A Mercedes car immediately following the Ghost car also came to a stop without impacting the Ghost car.  Ghost's understanding based on communications at the scene is that a Grand Cherokee traveling behind the Mercedes tried to swerve to avoid impacting the Mercedes but clipped the Mercedes and rolled over.  To Ghost's knowledge, nobody in the Grand Cherokee was injured.  The Ghost drivers heard a noise and then found that the rear bumper of the Ghost car had been damaged, which Ghost believes resulted from crash debris. The Ghost drivers drove the Ghost car back to the garage in conventional mode.

A Ghost ADS-equipped car was driving engaged Northbound on [XXX]. As always, there were two safety drivers in the car. Traffic was moving in all four lanes at or near the speed limit. The Ghost vehicle was in the right-middle lane. Another vehicle (the "lead" car) entered into the Ghost car's lane from the right. While there were numerous vehicles ahead of the Ghost car before that vehicle entered the lane, the lead vehicle blocked the view of the lane before it. Throughout the incident, traffic in the other three lanes continued at a steady pace.
When the lead vehicle entered into the lane ahead of the Ghost car, the Ghost car applied the brakes to accommodate the intrusion and then continued to match the speed of the lead car. The lead car suddenly slammed the brakes. The Ghost car followed suit, reducing its speed from 19 mps (42.5 mph) to 4.7 mps (10.5 mph) within the next three seconds. The Ghost car was able to match the rapid speed reduction of the lead car and avoid contact.
The driver following the Ghost car was not able to respond as quickly as the Ghost ADS. When the following driver began to apply the brakes, the car was about five meters behind and was traveling at 6.5 mps. At the point of impact, the Ghost vehicle was traveling at 4.78 mps (10.7 mph), matching the lead car. There were no injuries. The vehicles suffered only cosmetic damage, the drivers exchanged information, and the Ghost safety drivers returned to home base in conventional mode.

best_narrative:
On June [XXX], 2025, a Beep-operated Ford E-Transit (004D) equipped with an automated driving system developed by Oxa Autonomy Ltd. was involved in a collision with a third-party vehicle in Jacksonville, FL. 
While navigating northbound on [XXX], vehicle 004D approached the intersection of [XXX] under automated control. As vehicle 004D proceeded through the intersection, a 2020 Nissan Pathfinder navigating eastbound on [XXX] entered the intersection and struck vehicle 004D on the drivers door. Vehicle 004D had right of way through the intersection with no stop or yield the other vehicle had been stopped at a stop sign prior to the collision. There were no injuries reported at the scene, and both vehicles were driven away from the site of the collision. 
Prior to the collision, the safety operator in vehicle 004D stated that they noticed the other vehicle entering the intersection, applied the vehicles brakes, and steered away from the path of the other vehicle. Though they were able to bring vehicle 004D to a complete stop, they were unable to completely avoid the collision. Review of dashcam footage and of CCTV footage covering the intersection corroborate this narrative. The safety operator was referred for a post-accident drug and alcohol testing after the incident. They tested negative for any impairment. 
Analysis of the ADS logs determined that the ADS had detected the potential collision and had planned an emergency braking action. The safety operators intervention revoked control of the vehicle from the ADS preventing any automated braking actions. 
The [XXX] responded to the accident scene. The driver of the other vehicle was cited for failing to stop at the stop sign, and the JSO CSO noted that vehicle 004D was not at fault for the accident.

best_narrative:
On August [XXX], 2025, a Beep-operated Ford E-Transit (002B) equipped with an automated driving system developed by Oxa Autonomy Ltd. was involved in a collision with a stationary object in Jacksonville, FL.
While navigating westbound on [XXX] along a predefined route, vehicle 002B began to slowly veer towards the right curb. The vehicle eventually climbed the curb with the right front and rear wheels and the passenger-side mirror contacted a stationary light pole.
There was one passenger onboard at the time of the incident, an employee of the Jacksonville Transportation Authority. There were no reported injuries, and the vehicle was driven from the scene.
Review of the automated driving system logs as well as onboard video footage determined that the operator disengaged the automated driving system 7.5 seconds prior to the vehicle mounting the curb. Further examination of the onboard video footage noted that the operator appeared to be drowsy and inattentive prior to the incident.
Beep confirmed that the operators shift was in compliance with anti-fatigue policies for this operation. The operator was referred for a post-accident drug and alcohol testing following the incident and tested negative for any impairment. Following the conclusion of the investigation into this incident, the operator was separated from employment.

A Local Motors electric Olli shuttle (V1.0), equipped with the Robotic 
Research autonomy kit (enabling autonomous steering, acceleration, 
deceleration, and braking (AutoDrive)), was proceeding forward in autonomous 
mode through a parking lot in Yellowstone National Park. A parked passenger 
vehicle began reversing into the path of the Olli without signaling or yielding the right of way to the oncoming shuttle. The autonomy system detected the encroaching vehicle and commanded a braking event, which began deceleration of the Olli.  Though the Olli was nearly stopped by the time the of the accident, a collision still occurred, causing damage to the right rear of the passenger vehicle.  For reference this incident is under review with the Box-7 Team at NHTSA.

On January [XXX], 2026, a Beep-operated Karsan EJEST (BEEP-EJEST-0001) equipped with an automated driving system (ADS) develop by ADASTEC Corporation (ADASTEC) was involved in a collision with a third-party vehicle in Washington, D.C.

While traveling in autonomous mode westbound on [XXX] in the left-hand lane, the vehicle encountered a double-parked vehicle partially intruded into BEEP-EJEST-0001's lane of travel. BEEP-EJEST-0001 began to slow from its planned speed, and as decelerated, a human-driven Tesla Model Y (VEHICLE2) traveling parallel to BEEP-EJEST-0001 in the right-hand lane attempted to pull behind BEEP-EJEST-0001 to pass the double-parked vehicle in its lane of travel. VEHICLE2 encroached upon BEEP-EJEST-0001's predefined safety area of 5 cm, triggering an automated deceleration by BEEP-EJEST-0001 of approximately ÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂ2 m/s^2. VEHICLE2 began to further encroach upon BEEP-EJEST-0001's lane, triggering a higher-rate automated braking response consistent with system design. VEHICLE2 subsequently made contact with the rear of BEEP-EJEST-0001.

Beep confirmed that the operator performed his duties as trained and expected by BeepÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂs established policies and procedures. The vehicle operator was not referred for a post-accident drug and alcohol testing after the incident as the DOT criteria for such testing were not met. 

Available data indicated that VEHICLE2 followed BEEP-EJEST-0001 at a distance insufficient to respond to rapid deceleration and initiated a lane transition across a solid lane line immediately prior to contact.

BEEP-EJEST-0001 reacted as expected to the environment it encountered, slowing down to appropriate speeds and operated within its defined ODD at the time of the incident. There were (7) seven passengers on board. No injuries were reported. Both vehicles sustained minor damage, no airbags were deployed, and both vehicles were driven from the scene. No law enforcement attended to the scene. 

Following the incident, the vehicle was inspected for damages to the vehicle or sensors. It has been determined that any damage incurred was minor and did not impact the operational integrity of the vehicle.

The Beep shuttle attendant was traveling Southbound on [XXX] at approx 7-12 mph in ADS and headed towards the intersection of [XXX]. Beep vehicle stopped at red light at intersection. As the Beep vehicle was crossing the intersection in manual and slowing to a stop to return to ADS in a southbound direction, the trailing bus made light contact with its bicycle rack to the Beep shuttle rear end. The trailing vehicle is a bus owned and operated by the Pinellas Suncoast Transit Authority (PSTA). According to video, as reported by the shuttle attendant and the PSTA Road Supervisor, the bus was tailgating. 
 
Root Cause: Trailing vehicle following too closely and not observing Navya slowing to a stop.

On December [XXX], 2022, a Navya ARMA shuttle operated by Beep (VIN 1N9A1SACXJS[XXX]) was involved in a non-injury collision while operating in manual mode on [XXX] in Port Saint Lucie, FL.  While driving along [XXX] under autonomous control, the shuttle encountered a parked vehicle partially blocking its path, requiring disengagement of the autonomous drive system.  The safety driver attempted to manually drive the shuttle around the parked vehicle to reengage the autonomous drive system.  While manually steering the shuttle around the parked vehicle blocking its path, the shuttle failed to respond to manual control input and steered to the left, impacting a parked vehicle at a low rate of speed on the opposite side of [XXX].  The six passengers onboard alighted the shuttle, an accident report was filed with the local police, and the shuttle was removed from the scene.  Initial reports from the onboard safety driver indicated that while steering around the vehicle blocking the shuttle's path, the shuttle became unresponsive to control input.  The safety driver released the operator present button on the vehicle controller, which serves as dead man switch however, the vehicle failed to stop.  After the incident, further investigation by Navya confirmed that the root cause of the incident was a failure of the Xbox 360 controller used to manually control the vehicle.  Contact with the parked vehicle occurred between the time of failure and the vehicle drive system recognizing that the controller had failed an elapsed time of 4.05 seconds. In response to the incident, Beep suspended operation of all Navya ARMA vehicles pending identification and implementation of appropriate solutions to mitigate the risk of this failure leading to future incidents.
Subsequent investigation by Beep and Navya determined that the vehicle control software, NavyaDrive, is limited in its ability to monitor the validity of control signal input received from the Xbox 360 controller.  As the safety driver began to manually steer the vehicle, the controller failed, sending constant turning input to the vehicle drive system leading to the collision.  Though Navya developed software mitigations due to prior issues encountered with the controller, those mitigations were ineffective with respect to the failure leading to the incident on December [XXX].  Further, due to the short time between the controller failing and the collision (2.34sec), the safety driver was unable to identify the controller failure and trigger the emergency stop before the collision occurred.
In response to the incident, Navya is integrating a revised manual control system on the ARMA shuttle platform.  The revised manual control system utilizes industrial-grade components for improved electromechanical reliability and allows for active monitoring of the control system health, eliminating the root cause of the failure that led to the incident on December [XXX].  Software upgrades of all Navya ARMA vehicles will further reintroduce automated braking function while the ARMA vehicle is under manual control.  Beep is working with Navya to retrofit its fleet of ARMA vehicles with the revised manual control system as well as retrain and recertify its safety driver staff on the updated NavyaDrive software.
Discussion of the root cause analysis and solution design for the revised manual control system will be provided to NHTSA as a supplement to this report.

At Approximately 12:39 PM, the Beep Command Center (BCC) informed the LYNX dispatch center that another autonomous shuttle operating on the same route (VIN: VG9A2CB2CJV[XXX]) was being taken out of service due to insufficient battery charge to continue operation. The BCC later informed LYNX dispatch at 12:49PM that the second shuttle operating on route, P131, would also be returning to storage after the operator completed its current loop. At Approximately 12:52 PM, P131 approached the [XXX] stop within the designated Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) operating lane. The shuttle remained stopped for approximately two (2) and a half minutes while three (3) passengers alighted, and two (2) additional passengers boarded the shuttle. During that period, approximately two (2) minutes after stopping to service passengers, a LYNX bus pulled behind P131. Approximately thirty (30) seconds later, the LYNX bus exited the BRT lane and proceeded into the public travel lane alongside the BRT lane. As the LYNX bus entered the public travel lane, the safety driver onboard P131 engaged the autonomous drive system, and the shuttle proceeded in the BRT lane. After both vehicles had begun moving, P131 in the BRT lane and the LYNX bus in the public travel lane, the LYNX bus reentered the BRT lane encroaching on the shuttles path. The bus failed to provide adequate clearance and contacted the left front side of P131. Both vehicles stopped in the BRT lane after the collision. 
Within five (5) minutes of the collision, all passengers had safely alighted from the shuttle with no reported injuries and left the scene without giving statements. At approximately 2:20PM, the City of Orlando Police Department (OPD) arrived on scene to investigate the incident. Beep and LYNX supervisors were on scene to support the investigation. OPD collected statements from both the Beep safety driver and LYNX bus driver. The LYNX supervisor provided OPD access to the LYNX bus camera footage on scene, at which point the investigating officer made a verbal indication that the LYNX bus driver appeared to be at fault for the collision. 
Beeps safety driver was subsequently subjected to a standard post-collision drug and alcohol screening. 
1.The safety driver did not exhibit complete situational awareness and per their after-incident interview, failed to notice the bus prior to departing the station. Had the attendant continued to look and check behind/alongside P131 prior to departing the station,
the collision may have been avoided.
2.The safety driver attempted to stop the shuttle by disengaging the ADS using the HMI rather than using the emergency stop button. Use of the emergency stop would have increased the deceleration rate to 5 m/s2, decreasing the stopping distance of the shuttle. However, it is not clear whether use of the emergency stop would have fully avoided the collision given the point at which the LYNX LYMMO bus entered the BRT lane.
3.Deficiencies in the standard operating procedures (SOP) between LYNX and Beep allowed for a bus to be deployed onto the service at the same time as P131. Beep communicated to LYNX that both shuttles were exiting the service area. However, LYNX did not wait for final confirmation that both vehicles had left the route prior to dispatching the LYMMO bus involved in the incident, nor did LYNX notify Beep that a bus had been dispatched.
Additional information available upon request.

VIN # VG9A2CB2CIV[XXX] 
At approximately 5:54 p.m. on Wednesday, August [XXX], 2023, P84, travelling eastbound on [XXX], approached the three-way stop at [XXX] and [XXX]. P84 arrived at the intersection at the same time as two other vehicles: a grey van heading northbound on [XXX] and a red sedan heading westbound on [XXX]. The safety driver yielded to the northbound vehicle, which proceeded to make a right-hand turn eastbound onto [XXX]. After allowing the intersection to clear, the safety driver onboard P84 confirmed that it was safe for the shuttle to proceed through the intersection. As the shuttle entered the intersection, a second vehicle travelling northbound on [XXX], a white sedan, entered the intersection intending a left-hand turn onto westbound [XXX]. Both vehicles entered the intersection simultaneously, subsequently colliding at a low rate of speed in the middle of the intersection.  
At approximately 5:57 p.m., the Beep Command Center (BCC) was informed by the safety driver that P84 was involved in a minor collision with another vehicle at the intersection of [XXX] and [XXX]. The incident response protocol was initiated, and a supervisor dispatched to the scene. Local law enforcement (San Francisco Police Department) was contacted to report the collision but declined to respond given the minor nature of the collision, directing both parties to exchange information. After the Beep supervisor arrived on scene at approximately 6:49 p.m., the safety driver and supervisor removed the shuttle from the roadway and exchanged insurance information with the driver of the other vehicle. 
Damage to both vehicles was noted to be very minor. The shuttle sustained light scuffs, chips, and small paint transfer. The other vehicle sustained a small dent on the fender. Neither vehicle required towing, and both were safely driven from the scene. 
Beeps safety driver was subsequently subjected to a standard post-collision drug & alcohol screening which has since cleared.  
Contributing Factors 
Based on an analysis of the available evidence, Beep has determined the following factors may have contributed to the higher likelihood of a collision occurring in this instance: 
1.The white sedan failed to yield to P84 in accordance with California state law governing intersection priority.
2.The slight bend in P84s path to accommodate the lane shift through the intersection may have reduced the available stopping distance as the white sedan was not perceived as an obstacle in P84s path until it proceeded further into the intersection.
Incident Findings 
Beep developed the following findings regarding this incident:
1.P84 behaved as expected, detecting and maintaining awareness of the white sedan in the moments preceding the collision, commanding hard braking maneuver, and achieving a 3m/s2 deceleration rate once the white sedan encroached on P84s path.
2.The safety driver observed California state law and had the right of way when releasing P84 into the intersection.
3.The safety driver could have avoided the collision by using the emergency stop. The safety driver indicated that they were aware of the other vehicle through the duration of the incident and were aware of the proper procedure in this scenario but panicked in the moment.
Mitigation:
Implement a priority zone to account for the slight bend in the shuttles path and for future vehicles that may fail to yield.

At approximately 11:02 a.m. on Saturday, November [XXX], 2023, at [XXX] near [XXX] in the City of Orlando, a Beep-operated autonomous shuttle (P139) was involved in a collision with a City of Orlando LYNX bus resulting in moderate damage to the shuttle and minor damage to the Lynx bus. At approximately 10:55 AM CST on August [XXX], 2025, an Aurora truck was traveling westbound on [XXX] in autonomy during a development mission with an Aurora vehicle operator near Stephenville, Texas. 
The Aurora truck was in the right lane and approaching an unmarked right-lane closure with no advance warning signs. The Aurora vehicle operator decided to disengage autonomy and attempted to manually merge the Aurora truck into the left lane. During the vehicle operators manual lane change, a passing tractor trailer sped up on the left and made contact with the Aurora truck. The Aurora vehicle operator attempted to alert the other tractor trailer of the impact to the Aurora truck by honking the horn and flashing lights. However, the passing tractor trailer proceeded without stopping, and after being unable to gain the other drivers attention, the Aurora vehicle operator pulled over to the shoulder of the highway to assess damage to the Aurora truck. 
The Aurora vehicle operator reported damage to the trucks left side mirror and sensor equipment to Auroras dispatch before returning to Auroras terminal in Aledo, Texas. No injuries were reported.
